agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Orchestration-based saga for a 4-step courier marketplace order flow (Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the forward flow and, on any step failure, executes compensating transactions in reverse order. This design was chosen over choreography because the flow is strictly sequential, compensation ordering is critical, and observability of the saga state is a hard requirement for operations.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Owns the saga state machine. Drives forward steps sequentially, persists saga state to its own store after each transition, and triggers compensations in reverse on failure. Implements idempotency keys per step.","boundaries":"Knows step ordering and compensation mapping. Does NOT contain business logic for any individual step — only dispatches commands and interprets success/failure replies."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates order records (status: PENDING). Exposes CreateOrder command and CancelOrder compensation. Owns the Order aggregate.","boundaries":"Bounded to order lifecycle. Does not know about inventory, payment, or couriers."},{"name":"Inventory Service","responsibility":"Reserves inventory items for an order. Exposes ReserveInventory command and ReleaseInventory compensation.","boundaries":"Bounded to stock/inventory management. Tracks reservations with expiry TTLs as a safety net."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Charges the customer. Exposes ChargePayment command and RefundPayment compensation.","boundaries":"Bounded to payment processing. Interacts with external payment gateway. Must handle idempotent charge/refund."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Assigns a courier to the order. Exposes AssignCourier command and UnassignCourier compensation.","boundaries":"Bounded to courier availability and dispatch. Last step — its compensation is the first to fire on failure."},{"name":"Saga State Store","responsibility":"Durable persistence of saga instances — current step, step outcomes, idempotency keys, timestamps. Enables crash recovery.","boundaries":"Private to the Saga Orchestrator. Not shared with domain services."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Order Service","contract":"CreateOrder(saga_id, idempotency_key, order_details) → {order_id, status} | CancelOrder(saga_id, order_id) → {status}","coupling_type":"async command via message queue"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Inventory Service","contract":"ReserveInventory(saga_id, idempotency_key, order_id, items[]) → {reservation_id, status} | ReleaseInventory(saga_id, reservation_id) → {status}","coupling_type":"async command via message queue"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Payment Service","contract":"ChargePayment(saga_id, idempotency_key, order_id, amount, payment_method) → {payment_id, status} | RefundPayment(saga_id, payment_id) → {status}","coupling_type":"async command via message queue"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Courier Service","contract":"AssignCourier(saga_id, idempotency_key, order_id, pickup_location, delivery_location) → {assignment_id, status} | UnassignCourier(saga_id, assignment_id) → {status}","coupling_type":"async command via message queue"}],"data_flow":"1. Client submits order → API Gateway → Saga Orchestrator creates saga instance (state: STARTED). 2. Orchestrator sends CreateOrder → Order Service replies success → state: ORDER_CREATED. 3. Orchestrator sends ReserveInventory → Inventory Service replies success → state: INVENTORY_RESERVED. 4. Orchestrator sends ChargePayment → Payment Service replies success → state: PAYMENT_CHARGED. 5. Orchestrator sends AssignCourier → Courier Service replies success → state: COMPLETED. On failure at any step N, orchestrator transitions to COMPENSATING and issues compensations for steps N-1 down to 1 in reverse order. Final state: COMPENSATED or FAILED_COMPENSATION (triggers alert).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestration Saga","rationale":"Sequential steps with strict compensation ordering. Orchestrator centralizes the state machine, making the flow explicit, testable, and observable. Choreography would scatter the flow across 4 services, making compensation ordering fragile and debugging difficult."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Commands","rationale":"Network retries and at-least-once delivery mean every command (forward and compensating) must be idempotent. Each command carries an idempotency_key derived from saga_id + step, and each service deduplicates on it."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine with Durable Persistence","rationale":"Persisting state after each transition enables crash recovery. On restart, the orchestrator reloads incomplete sagas and resumes from the last persisted state — either continuing forward or resuming compensation."},{"pattern":"Reservation with Expiry TTL","rationale":"Inventory reservations carry a TTL (e.g., 10 minutes). If the saga stalls and compensation never arrives, the reservation auto-expires. Defense-in-depth against stuck sagas."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId, states: PENDING → CONFIRMED → CANCELLED)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["StockItem (root: SKU)", "Reservation (root: ReservationId, states: HELD → COMMITTED → RELEASED, has expiry TTL)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: PaymentId, states: PENDING → CHARGED → REFUNDED)"]},{"name":"Courier Dispatch","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAssignment (root: AssignmentId, states: ASSIGNED → ACTIVE → UNASSIGNED)","Courier (root: CourierId, availability state)"]},{"name":"Saga Coordination","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["SagaInstance (root: SagaId, states: STARTED → ORDER_CREATED → INVENTORY_RESERVED → PAYMENT_CHARGED → COMPLETED | COMPENSATING → COMPENSATED | FAILED_COMPENSATION)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Orchestrator issues commands, Order Service fulfills them. Order Service owns its model; Orchestrator only knows command/reply contracts."},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier with ACL — Payment Service wraps external payment gateway behind an Anti-Corruption Layer."},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Courier Dispatch","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestration over Choreography","gains":"Explicit flow control, centralized saga state for observability, deterministic compensation ordering, easier to test end-to-end","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination single point — must be highly available. Slight coupling: orchestrator knows step ordering.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga with each service emitting events and listening for predecessors — rejected because compensation ordering becomes implicit and error-prone with 4 sequential steps","Hybrid: choreography for forward flow, orchestration for compensation — rejected as unnecessarily complex"]},{"decision":"Async message queues over synchronous HTTP","gains":"Temporal decoupling, natural retry semantics, at-least-once delivery, services can be independently scaled","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure complexity (message broker), eventual consistency, harder local development setup","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous HTTP chain — rejected because a single slow/down service blocks the entire chain and retry logic becomes ad-hoc"]},{"decision":"Saga state persisted after every transition","gains":"Crash recovery, auditability, operational visibility into stuck sagas","sacrifices":"Write amplification — one DB write per step transition (acceptable at typical order volumes)","alternatives_considered":["Persist only on completion/failure — rejected because crash mid-saga would leave orphaned reservations/charges with no recovery path"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new saga steps","evolution_path":"The state machine is a linear sequence. Adding a step (e.g., Fraud Check between Payment and Courier) requires adding one forward command, one compensation command, and two state transitions. No changes to existing services."},{"area":"Compensation retry policies","evolution_path":"Each compensation step can have its own retry policy (exponential backoff, max attempts) configured in the orchestrator. Can be tuned per-service without code changes."},{"area":"Parallel steps in future","evolution_path":"If business rules change and some steps can run in parallel (e.g., inventory + payment simultaneously), the state machine can be extended to a DAG. The orchestrator pattern supports this — choreography would not."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Step ordering","reason":"The business rule mandates this exact sequence: Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier. Compensation must be the exact reverse. This is intentionally rigid — changing the order is a business decision, not an engineering one."},{"area":"Idempotency keys","reason":"Every service MUST implement idempotent command handling. This is non-negotiable for correctness. Removing idempotency breaks at-least-once delivery guarantees."},{"area":"Saga state schema","reason":"The saga state machine schema is a critical contract. Migrations must be backward-compatible since in-flight sagas may span schema changes."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensation failure — a compensating action (e.g., RefundPayment) itself fails","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement compensations as idempotent and retriable with exponential backoff. After max retries, transition saga to FAILED_COMPENSATION state and alert operations team. Include a manual resolution dashboard. Payment refunds should also have a dead-letter queue for manual processing."},{"risk":"Saga orchestrator unavailability","severity":"high","mitigation":"Run orchestrator as a stateless service with durable state in the database. Multiple instances can process different sagas concurrently. Use distributed locks or partition sagas by ID to prevent double-processing."},{"risk":"Stuck sagas — orchestrator crashes between issuing a command and receiving the reply","severity":"high","mitigation":"On startup, scan for sagas in non-terminal states older than a threshold. Re-issue the last command (idempotent, so safe). Inventory TTL provides a safety net for the reservation step specifically."},{"risk":"Message ordering and duplication from the broker","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Idempotency keys on every command handle duplicates. Saga state machine rejects out-of-order replies (e.g., a late reply to a step that has already been compensated)."},{"risk":"Inventory reservation TTL expires before saga completes","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Set TTL conservatively (e.g., 10 min) well above expected saga duration. Monitor saga duration p99. If approaching TTL, extend the reservation or alert."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga orchestrator as a state machine backed by a durable store (Postgres recommended). Use a message broker (RabbitMQ, SQS, or Kafka) for async command/reply between orchestrator and services. Each service implements two endpoints: a forward command and a compensating command, both idempotent. The saga instance table should track: saga_id, current_state, step_results (JSON), created_at, updated_at, idempotency_keys. A background sweeper process recovers stuck sagas. Start with a simple linear state machine — do not over-engineer with a generic saga framework unless you have 3+ distinct saga flows.","files_to_create_or_modify":["saga-orchestrator/src/state-machine.ts — Saga state machine definition (states, transitions, compensation map)","saga-orchestrator/src/saga-runner.ts — Drives the state machine: dispatches commands, handles replies, persists state","saga-orchestrator/src/recovery.ts — Startup sweeper for stuck sagas","saga-orchestrator/src/saga-store.ts — Repository for SagaInstance aggregate (Postgres)","order-service/src/handlers/create-order.ts — Idempotent CreateOrder command handler","order-service/src/handlers/cancel-order.ts — CancelOrder compensation handler","inventory-service/src/handlers/reserve-inventory.ts — Idempotent ReserveInventory with TTL","inventory-service/src/handlers/release-inventory.ts — ReleaseInventory compensation","payment-service/src/handlers/charge-payment.ts — Idempotent ChargePayment with gateway ACL","payment-service/src/handlers/refund-payment.ts — RefundPayment compensation","courier-service/src/handlers/assign-courier.ts — Idempotent AssignCourier","courier-service/src/handlers/unassign-courier.ts — UnassignCourier compensation"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each command handler: 1) Check idempotency key — if already processed, return cached result. 2) Execute business logic. 3) Persist result + idempotency key atomically (same transaction). 4) Publish reply. Compensations follow the same pattern. The saga state machine should be a pure function: (currentState, event) → (nextState, command) — easy to unit test without infrastructure.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use distributed transactions (2PC) across services — they do not scale and create tight coupling","Do NOT skip idempotency — without it, retries cause double charges, double reservations, etc.","Do NOT embed compensation logic inside forward handlers — keep them as separate, independently deployable handlers","Do NOT use synchronous HTTP calls from the orchestrator to services — a single slow service will cascade timeouts","Do NOT assume compensations always succeed — design for compensation failure with retries and dead-letter handling","Do NOT share a database across services — each service owns its data; the orchestrator has its own saga store"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestration saga is the right pattern for a strict 4-step sequential flow with ordered compensation","Idempotency and durable state persistence are non-negotiable for correctness","Async messaging decouples services appropriately","Compensation ordering (reverse of forward steps) matches the stated business rule"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific message broker choice depends on existing infrastructure (Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS — all viable)","Inventory TTL value (10 min suggested) needs tuning based on real saga duration metrics","Whether Courier Dispatch is truly 'core' or 'supporting' depends on business differentiation strategy"],"assumptions":["Each service is independently deployed with its own database","A message broker is available or can be introduced","Order volumes are moderate (not requiring event sourcing or CQRS for the saga store)","The four steps are strictly sequential — no parallelism in the current business rules"]}}

A central Saga Orchestrator drives the forward flow and, on any step failure, executes compensating transactions in reverse order. content.thinking:
Let me design a saga pattern for this courier marketplace order flow.

Classification: `none` — sufficient constraints exist. We have clear steps, clear failure/compensation rules, and a well-defined domain.

A central Saga Orchestrator drives the forward flow and, on any step failure, executes compensating transactions in reverse order. This design was chosen over choreography because the flow is strictly sequential, compensation ordering is critical, and observability of the saga state is a hard requirement for operations.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Owns the saga state machine. Does NOT contain business logic for any individual step — only dispatches commands and interprets success/failure replies."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates order records (status: PENDING). Client submits order → API Gateway → Saga Orchestrator creates saga instance (state: STARTED). 2. Orchestrator sends CreateOrder → Order Service replies success → state: ORDER_CREATED. 3. Orchestrator sends ReserveInventory → Inventory Service replies success → state: INVENTORY_RESERVED. 4. Orchestrator sends ChargePayment → Payment Service replies success → state: PAYMENT_CHARGED. 5. Orchestrator sends AssignCourier → Courier Service replies success → state: COMPLETED. On failure at any step N, orchestrator transitions to COMPENSATING and issues compensations for steps N-1 down to 1 in reverse order. Final state: COMPENSATED or FAILED_COMPENSATION (triggers alert).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestration Saga","rationale":"Sequential steps with strict compensation ordering. Orchestrator centralizes the state machine, making the flow explicit, testable, and observable. Choreography would scatter the flow across 4 services, making compensation ordering fragile and debugging difficult."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Commands","rationale":"Network retries and at-least-once delivery mean every command (forward and compensating) must be idempotent. Each command carries an idempotency_key derived from saga_id + step, and each service deduplicates on it."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine with Durable Persistence","rationale":"Persisting state after each transition enables crash recovery. On restart, the orchestrator reloads incomplete sagas and resumes from the last persisted state — either continuing forward or resuming compensation."},{"pattern":"Reservation with Expiry TTL","rationale":"Inventory reservations carry a TTL (e.g., 10 minutes). If the saga stalls and compensation never arrives, the reservation auto-expires. Defense-in-depth against stuck sagas."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId, states: PENDING → CONFIRMED → CANCELLED)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["StockItem (root: SKU)", "Reservation (root: ReservationId, states: HELD → COMMITTED → RELEASED, has expiry TTL)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: PaymentId, states: PENDING → CHARGED → REFUNDED)"]},{"name":"Courier Dispatch","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAssignment (root: AssignmentId, states: ASSIGNED → ACTIVE → UNASSIGNED)","Courier (root: CourierId, availability state)"]},{"name":"Saga Coordination","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["SagaInstance (root: SagaId, states: STARTED → ORDER_CREATED → INVENTORY_RESERVED → PAYMENT_CHARGED → COMPLETED | COMPENSATING → COMPENSATED | FAILED_COMPENSATION)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Orchestrator issues commands, Order Service fulfills them. Order Service owns its model; Orchestrator only knows command/reply contracts."},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier with ACL — Payment Service wraps external payment gateway behind an Anti-Corruption Layer."},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Courier Dispatch","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestration over Choreography","gains":"Explicit flow control, centralized saga state for observability, deterministic compensation ordering, easier to test end-to-end","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination single point — must be highly available. Slight coupling: orchestrator knows step ordering.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga with each service emitting events and listening for predecessors — rejected because compensation ordering becomes implicit and error-prone with 4 sequential steps","Hybrid: choreography for forward flow, orchestration for compensation — rejected as unnecessarily complex"]},{"decision":"Async message queues over synchronous HTTP","gains":"Temporal decoupling, natural retry semantics, at-least-once delivery, services can be independently scaled","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure complexity (message broker), eventual consistency, harder local development setup","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous HTTP chain — rejected because a single slow/down service blocks the entire chain and retry logic becomes ad-hoc"]},{"decision":"Saga state persisted after every transition","gains":"Crash recovery, auditability, operational visibility into stuck sagas","sacrifices":"Write amplification — one DB write per step transition (acceptable at typical order volumes)","alternatives_considered":["Persist only on completion/failure — rejected because crash mid-saga would leave orphaned reservations/charges with no recovery path"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new saga steps","evolution_path":"The state machine is a linear sequence. Adding a step (e.g., Fraud Check between Payment and Courier) requires adding one forward command, one compensation command, and two state transitions. No changes to existing services."},{"area":"Compensation retry policies","evolution_path":"Each compensation step can have its own retry policy (exponential backoff, max attempts) configured in the orchestrator. Can be tuned per-service without code changes."},{"area":"Parallel steps in future","evolution_path":"If business rules change and some steps can run in parallel (e.g., inventory + payment simultaneously), the state machine can be extended to a DAG. Use a message broker (RabbitMQ, SQS, or Kafka) for async command/reply between orchestrator and services. Each service implements two endpoints: a forward command and a compensating command, both idempotent. The saga instance table should track: saga_id, current_state, step_results (JSON), created_at, updated_at, idempotency_keys. A background sweeper process recovers stuck sagas. Compensations follow the same pattern. The saga state machine should be a pure function: (currentState, event) → (nextState, command) — easy to unit test without infrastructure.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use distributed transactions (2PC) across services — they do not scale and create tight coupling","Do NOT skip idempotency — without it, retries cause double charges, double reservations, etc.","Do NOT embed compensation logic inside forward handlers — keep them as separate, independently deployable handlers","Do NOT use synchronous HTTP calls from the orchestrator to services — a single slow service will cascade timeouts","Do NOT assume compensations always succeed — design for compensation failure with retries and dead-letter handling","Do NOT share a database across services — each service owns its data; the orchestrator has its own saga store"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestration saga is the right pattern for a strict 4-step sequential flow with ordered compensation","Idempotency and durable state persistence are non-negotiable for correctness","Async messaging decouples services appropriately","Compensation ordering (reverse of forward steps) matches the stated business rule"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific message broker choice depends on existing infrastructure (Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS — all viable)","Inventory TTL value (10 min suggested) needs tuning based on real saga duration metrics","Whether Courier Dispatch is truly 'core' or 'supporting' depends on business differentiation strategy"],"assumptions":["Each service is independently deployed with its own database","A message broker is available or can be introduced","Order volumes are moderate (not requiring event sourcing or CQRS for the saga store)","The four steps are strictly sequential — no parallelism in the current business rules"]}}
